description: Formal scope definition for the current pipeline stage. Loaded by analysis scripts to ensure downstream work does not conflate scene-individuation consistency with attribute-classification consistency.
directive_reminder: See AI_DIRECTIVE.md at the repo root. This is Stage 1: scene individuation only. Do NOT resolve attribute-level (illusion vs incrusted, object-class, etc.) disagreements — those are deferred to a future Stage 2. The rater's subjective judgement about what is a hallucinatory scene is the primary data.
primary_question: Did both raters individuate the same narrative passage as a hallucinatory scene?

what_individuation_means.definition: Scene individuation is the atomic act of pointing to a passage in a trip report and asserting 'a hallucinatory scene happened here'. It is the precursor to every other coding step — before a rater can classify a scene's modality, object class, modal status, dynamics, or illusion-vs-incrusted tag, they must first decide the passage is a scene at all.

individuation_vs_classification.individuation: Stage 1 (this project). Dichotomous decision: is this passage a hallucinatory scene, yes or no? The more OBJECTIVE of the two steps. Grounded in the PDF 'Guidelines for individuating hallucinations in trip reports'.
individuation_vs_classification.classification: Stage 2 (deferred). Multi-attribute taxonomic decision: given a scene exists, what kind of hallucination is it (illusion / incrusted / detached / immersive; Human / Artefact / Animal / ...; Possible / Impossible; etc.). The more SUBJECTIVE step — rater taxonomic judgement dominates here.

what_individuation_means.why_individuation_is_more_objective: Two raters can still draw the 'scene vs not-scene' line differently — individuation is not perfectly objective — but the disagreement is of a different kind than classification disagreement. The subjective residue in individuation is exactly what Stage 1 measures. Classification subjectivity is preserved in the data untouched and deferred to Stage 2.

core_analytical_question.question: For every narrative passage individuated by only ONE of the two raters, classify the discrepancy into one of THREE distinct classes: MISS, GRANULARITY, or AMBIGUITY (subdivided 2a / 2b).

verdicts.MISS: By this rater's OBSERVED criterion (established from the _AB scenes of the same trip and coder pair), they should have individuated this passage too — they simply overlooked it. Rater-compliance gap; resolvable by rater reconciliation per Project description §7. No instrument change needed.
verdicts.GRANULARITY: Raters agree something happened in this narrative region but split it differently: one rater individuated a holistic scene; the other individuated its component sub-scenes (or vice versa). NOT an ambiguity — resolved at the data layer by the granularity coding rule below. The larger-scope (more conservative) individuation becomes the canonical parent; the fragment's parent_scene_id points to it. Downstream analysis may collapse fragments into the parent and pool the attributes of both raters.
verdicts.AMBIGUITY_2a: The PDF Guidelines under-specify the edge case. Both individuation and omission are defensible readings. The Guidelines need a clarifying rule that decides, one way or the other, whether passages of this type are hallucinatory scenes. Instruction-design gap; resolvable only by rewriting the Guidelines. Deferred.
verdicts.AMBIGUITY_2b: The passage is phenomenologically real but not a hallucinatory scene under any reasonable reading. It is a different phenomenon (sensation, mood shift, dissociative state, inner imagery/visualised insight, ego-dissolution metaphor, existential belief, altered somatosensory phenomenology, post-trip cognitive residue). The taxonomy needs a new non-hallucinatory-scene category. Instruction-design gap of a different kind. Deferred.

granularity_coding_rule.trigger: Solo scene's canonical span overlaps an _AB scene or an opposite-rater solo scene in the same trip, OR the driver was auto-classified as _FRAG.
granularity_coding_rule.default_resolution: The larger-scope individuation is the parent. Usually the _AB shared scene is the parent because both raters agreed at that level of scope; the fragment is the minority split. The fragment scene keeps its scene_id but sets parent_scene_id to the parent's scene_id. Driver suffix _FRAG marks these structurally.
granularity_coding_rule.when_in_doubt: Prefer the conservative (larger-scope) reading. The encompassing scene takes precedence.
granularity_coding_rule.preserves_rater_judgement: This is a decision rule for downstream aggregation, not adjudication of rater judgement. The fragment rater's individuation is preserved — scene_id, rater_status, canonical_desc, and spans remain untouched. Only the canonical scope for analysis aggregation is committed.
downstream_options: collapse fragments into parent and pool both raters' attributes onto the parent scene
retain fragments as annotated sub-units of the parent scene

how_to_judge_miss_vs_ambiguity.principle: The 'truth' against which a solo scene is judged is NOT the PDF Guidelines read in isolation and applied from scratch. It is each rater's DEMONSTRATED application of the PDF Guidelines on the scenes both raters individuated. The shared (_AB) scenes reveal each rater's OPERATIONAL criterion — 'scene' as the two raters actually used the term in this coding effort.
procedure: 1. Establish each rater's observed criterion from the _AB scenes of the same trip (and same coder pair).
2. Read the solo scene in context: narrative passage, rater's canonical excerpt, stage1_rationale.

3. Apply the observed criterion. If the solo passage matches the kind of content both raters individuated on the shared scenes and one rater simply did not annotate it → MISS. If the solo passage sits at content the shared scenes do NOT establish a clear rule for (ambient amplification, thought/memory/metaphor, somatic transformation, fragment-of-holistic) → AMBIGUITY; decide whether flavour 2a (clarify existing rule) or 2b (add new non-scene category).
how_to_judge_miss_vs_ambiguity.why_shared_scenes_not_pdf_in_abstract: The PDF is not self-interpreting. Two competent raters reading the same PDF can land at different operational criteria — that IS how inter-rater disagreement arises. Using each rater's _AB scenes as the reference grounds the judgement in what they actually did, not in a theoretical ideal. A MISS verdict then means 'by the criterion this rater used elsewhere in this trip, they should have individuated this too' — a defensible claim anchored in observed behaviour rather than adjudicator preference.
how_to_judge_miss_vs_ambiguity.how_drivers_relate_to_verdicts: The driver suffixes are the first-pass expression of this procedure. _FRAG / _AMP / _AMB / _SOMA are content types where the Guidelines are systematically under-specified → strong AMBIGUITY candidates. _RCL scenes have no automatic rule fit → these need human MISS-vs-AMBIGUITY adjudication against the trip's own _AB scenes.

core_analytical_question.what_this_tells_us: Generalising from what both raters agreed on gives the consistent core of the dataset. Classifying the drivers of disagreement as MISS / AMBIGUITY-2a / AMBIGUITY-2b tells us whether remaining inconsistency is a rater-compliance problem (solvable by reconciliation) or an instruction-design problem (solvable only by clarifying or extending the coding instructions). This is the ONE question the project exists to answer at this stage.
core_analytical_question.why_stated_explicitly: Any AI agent working on this project must keep this analytical question central. All infrastructure (scene-ID taxonomy, driver suffixes, annotated trip-report pages, visualizations, pretext renderer, etc.) exists in service of answering it. If an action under consideration does not help answer it, re-read this section.

rater_subjective_judgement_is_primary_da: The rater's subjective judgement about what counts as a hallucinatory scene is the primary data. Any analysis of agreement must be anchored in what the raters actually individuated, not in what we think they should have individuated. Do not silently drop, merge, or override rater individuations. Classify the discrepancy driver instead.
in_scope: Whether rater A individuated a narrative passage as a hallucinatory scene
Whether rater B individuated the same passage as a hallucinatory scene
Whether both raters' individuations refer to the same narrative passage (canonical_span overlap)
Classifying the *reason* a passage was individuated by only one rater (the driver taxonomy: FRAG / AMP / AMB / SOMA / RCL)
explicitly_out_of_scope: Whether a scene is classified as 'Illusion' vs 'Hallucination of an incrusted object' vs 'Hallucination of a detached object' vs 'Full-blown immersive hallucination' (the four leaves under 'Type of visual alteration')
Which object-class tags (Human / Artefact / Animal / Plant / Inorganic entity / Extraordinary entity and their sub-leaves) each rater attached to a scene
Which modality tags (Visual / Auditory / Tactile / Olfactory / Gustatory / Nonsensory) each rater attached
Which 'Modal status' leaf was selected (Possible and probable / Possible but improbable / Impossible)
Which 'Dynamics' tag was applied (Object constancy vs No object constancy)

Whether an observation was placed at scene-level vs trip-level — Stage 1 treats the rater's placement as their honest reading; a scene is a scene if the rater individuated it, regardless of the taxonomic fit of the attributes they chose
rationale: The Guidelines for individuating hallucinations in trip reports.pdf defines the atomic coding unit as the hallucinatory scene: 'every hallucinatory scene should be coded as a hallucination'. Inter-rater reliability at this atomic unit — do both raters see the same narrative passage as a scene — is logically prior to any comparison of what they tagged within or about that scene. Stage 1 therefore measures only scene individuation. Attribute-classification decisions made by each rater (illusion vs incrusted etc.) are recorded alongside each scene for transparency but are understood to reflect subjective rater judgement and are not resolved, adjudicated, or corrected at this stage.

driver_taxonomy.purpose: Classify WHY only one rater individuated a given narrative passage as a scene. The driver suffix on scene_id is a diagnostic, not a resolution. A scene flagged _AMP / _AMB / _SOMA / _FRAG / _RCL remains fully in the dataset; the rater's individuation decision is preserved as-is.

_FRAG.scope: one rater's sub-scene of the other's holistic scene
_FRAG.action: linked to parent_scene_id; both raters agreed something happened in this narrative region

_AMP.scope: ambient perceptual amplification without a discrete object
_AMP.action: flagged; Case-2 borderline; one rater expansively individuated, the other conservatively omitted

_AMB.scope: thought / memory / metaphor content without explicit hallucinatory object
_AMB.action: flagged; likely trip-level phenomenon per PDF but one rater individuated at scene-level

_SOMA.action: flagged; body-schema / ego-dissolution content that one rater individuated as a scene

_RCL.scope: no automatic match to other drivers — likely genuine miss by the other rater
_RCL.action: flagged for human reconciliation per Project description.pdf §7

driver_taxonomy.note: Driver categories relate to *whether a passage should have been individuated at all*, not to how its attributes were classified. A scene rated _AB (both raters individuated) may still carry widely different attribute tags from rater A vs rater B; those attribute disagreements are recorded in data/agreement_flags.csv and are not resolved by Stage 1.

stage2_scope_note: A future Stage 2 analysis would address attribute-classification consistency on shared (_AB) scenes — for example, quantifying how often both raters agree on 'Type of visual alteration' given they agreed a scene exists. That work is OUT OF SCOPE for the current pipeline. The data-structure framework already supports it: agreement_flags.csv gives per-(scene, item) agreement flags for every attribute on every shared scene.

authoritative_sources.coding_instructions: ../Coding Instructions/Guidelines for individuating hallucinations in trip reports.pdf
authoritative_sources.taxonomy_source: ../Coding Instructions/Description of items to be coded.pdf
authoritative_sources.project_motivation: ../Coding Instructions/The phenomenology of hallucinogenic experiences - Project description.pdf
